title: SEGMENT 1 — The long-read problem: what short reads can't do

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — The long-read problem: what short reads can't do
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.
Today and for the next two hours, we're going to talk about PacBio sequencing — or, to give it its full name, single-molecule real-time sequencing on SMRT cells, with HiFi reads as the modern output.

And I want to begin not with the technology, but with the problem the technology solves — because PacBio only makes sense if you first understand what was missing from the sequencing world it walked into.
So let's set the scene.
By the late 2000s, the genomics field had a winning machine: Illumina.
Short, accurate reads, generated by the millions, dirt cheap per base.
If you wanted to count RNA transcripts, find single-nucleotide variants, do a GWAS study on a hundred thousand people, Illumina was unbeatable and remains unbeatable today.
Tens of billions of bases for the cost of a nice dinner.
The short-read revolution had arrived, and it had transformed biology.
But there was a problem.
The reads were short.
A hundred and fifty bases, two hundred and fifty bases at the high end.
Beautiful little postage stamps of sequence.

And it turns out that when you try to assemble a genome out of postage stamps — when you try to reconstruct an entire chromosome from billions of tiny overlapping fragments — there are vast regions of the genome where this just doesn't work.
Let me give you the analogy.
Imagine you've shredded a book — not the kind of shred that gives you long ribbons, but the confetti shred, the secure document destruction shred, where every piece is about the size of a fingernail.
Now imagine I hand you a wheelbarrow of those confetti pieces and ask you to reconstruct the book.
You can do it, in principle, by matching the edges of pieces against each other and finding overlaps.
And if the book is written in ordinary prose, where most sentences are unique, you can probably reassemble most of it.
But what if the book contains a passage where the same paragraph is repeated three hundred times in a row?
What if there's a chapter that's just the word "the" printed ten thousand times?
You'll find lots of confetti with "the" on it, but you'll have no way to know which "the" goes where in the sequence.
The pieces aren't long enough to span the repetitive region.
The book has, in effect, a hole in it that your confetti can't reconstruct.
That's exactly the situation short-read sequencing finds itself in when it hits the repetitive parts of a genome.
And the human genome — and most genomes — are full of repetitive parts.
Centromeres, those big chunks of repetitive DNA at the middle of every chromosome, are essentially un-assemblable from short reads.
Telomeres at the chromosome ends, made of the same six bases repeated thousands of times — un-assemblable.
Tandem repeats inside genes, the kind that cause Huntington's disease and fragile X syndrome when they expand — un-measurable beyond a certain length.
Segmental duplications, where megabase-sized chunks of the genome have been copied to other locations — short reads can't tell which copy is which.
Whole classes of structural variation — large inversions, large deletions, complex rearrangements — just slip through the cracks of short-read pipelines because the events are bigger than the reads.
The Human Genome Project, finished officially in 2003, was missing about eight percent of the genome — and that missing eight percent wasn't randomly distributed.
It was concentrated exactly in those repetitive regions where short reads can't reach.
For nearly two decades, the human reference genome had holes in it, and those holes were the most genetically interesting and dynamic parts of our chromosomes.
The solution, in principle, is obvious.
If short reads can't span a five-thousand-base repeat, use ten-thousand-base reads.
If ten thousand isn't enough, use fifty thousand.
If fifty thousand isn't enough, use a hundred thousand.
Make the confetti longer than the repetitive passage, and the problem goes away.
You can reassemble the book.
But making long reads turns out to be technically vicious.

Every chemistry that researchers had used for sequencing — Sanger's chain termination, Illumina's bridge amplification, the various other short-read methods — was built around bulk biochemistry, where you take a population of identical DNA molecules and read them collectively.

As reads get longer, the chemistry gets noisier; molecules in the population drift out of phase with each other, signal-to-noise collapses, and by the time you're trying to read a kilobase, you've lost the signal.
Short reads weren't a stylistic choice; they were a consequence of bulk methods.
Reading long required a fundamentally different approach.
And that's what PacBio brought to the table.
Not an incremental improvement on existing chemistry, but a wholly different physical paradigm.
Instead of reading many molecules in bulk, watch one molecule, one polymerase, in real time, and let it tell you the sequence as it copies.
Drop the bulk averaging entirely.
Single molecule.
One at a time.
Real time observation.
That's the bet.
And the bet, after about fifteen years of struggle, paid off.
So here's our contract for the next two hours.

If you walk out of here at the end of the lecture and you can do three things — one, sketch a zero-mode waveguide and explain why a hole smaller than a wavelength of light lets us observe one polymerase at a time; two, walk through what a SMRTbell template is and how it produces HiFi reads of ninety-nine point nine percent accuracy out of fifteen percent raw error; three, look at a modern genome assembly paper — telomere-to-telomere, structural variant catalog, full-length transcript
characterization — and tell me why PacBio specifically was the right tool for the job — then we've succeeded.
Everything else hangs off those three.
We'll meet the rough commercial history along the way.
PacBio, the company, had a famously rocky start.
The first instrument was expensive, the early data was disappointing, the financials were ugly for years, and there was a period in the mid-2010s when the company looked like it might not survive.
Then in 2019 they released the Sequel II, in 2020 they released the HiFi workflow as standard, and within two years they had become the gold standard for serious genome assembly.
The technology that was nearly abandoned became the technology that finished the human genome.
We'll get to that story in segments ten and thirteen.
And throughout the lecture, I'll be comparing PacBio against its main long-read competitor, Oxford Nanopore.
The two platforms have a deep rivalry, they have different physical principles, different strengths, different weaknesses, and you can't really understand one without the other.
Think of them as the two parallel solutions to the same problem — both born in roughly the same era, both attacking single-molecule sequencing, both producing long reads.
PacBio is the precision tool.
Nanopore is the speed-and-length champion.
We'll see why throughout.
Now let's go meet the idea itself.
